points.text: COVID-19 is a contagious disease caused by the coronavirus SARS‑CoV‑2. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00000]
The disease emerged in January 2020 and spread worldwide, leading to the COVID‑19 pandemic. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00000]
The WHO declared COVID‑19 a global health emergency in March 2020 and ended the emergency in May 2023. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00000]
The official name COVID‑19 (short for “coronavirus disease 2019”) and the virus name SARS‑CoV‑2 were issued by WHO on 11 Feb 2020. [doc1_covid19.txt:chunk_00009
COVID-19 is an illness caused by the virus SARS‑CoV‑2. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00000]
The virus spreads mainly through close contact via tiny respiratory droplets in the air. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00000]
Transmission can also occur when infected people cough, sneeze, breathe, sing or talk, releasing virus‑laden breath. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00007]
Many infected individuals have mild illness or no symptoms, but older adults and those with certain conditions may require hospital care or die. [doc2_covid19.t
High‑risk groups include people 65+, infants under 6 months, and those with heart disease, diabetes, chronic organ disease, obesity, smoking, etc. [doc2_covid19
Good indoor airflow, physical distancing, mask wearing, and hygiene help prevent spread. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00001]
Vaccination (e.g., Moderna, Pfizer‑BioNTech, Novavax) reduces serious illness, hospitalisation, and death. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00017][doc2_covid19.txt:chunk
COVID‑19 can be contracted more than once as immunity may wane and viral mutations can evade prior protection. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00009][doc2_covid19.txt:c
The virus can infect some pets (cats, dogs, hamsters, ferrets), though transmission from pets to people is rare. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00010]
Surface transmission is rare; infection from contaminated surfaces like public bathrooms is uncommon. [doc2_covid19.txt:chunk_00012]
COVID-19 is an infectious respiratory illness caused by the coronavirus SARS‑CoV‑2.
